docs.title: Dybee Documentation
docs.text: Dybee is a cloud-based platform for building energy management and continuous commissioning. It combines energy monitoring, digital twin modeling, real-time data acquisition, and automated fault detection in a single environment.

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text: I want to… Go to Get started with a new building Building Setup Understand core concepts Key Concepts Set up energy monitoring Energy Sources Model building systems Building Modeling Write a formula Functions Reference Review detected faults Fault Detection Build a dashboard Dashboard Structure

docs.title: KPIs
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What is a KPI?
docs.text:
A KPI (Key Performance Indicator) is a numeric metric calculated hourly for each entity in a family. Unlike rules (which return 0 or 1), KPIs return quantitative values — efficiency ratios, energy totals, temperature differentials, etc.

KPIs are the primary building blocks for dashboard widgets and cross-entity analysis.

docs.title: Creating KPIs
docs.text:
KPIs are defined on a family in Modeling → Families → KPIs. Each KPI requires:

Field Description Name Display label Alias Short code used in formula references Formula Calculation expression Unit Display unit (°C, kWh, %, etc.)

docs.title: Referencing KPIs in Formulas
docs.text:
KPIs can be referenced in other formulas using:

[EntityName].kpi.ALIAS

Or, within a family rule or KPI using hierarchy navigation:

kpi.ALIAS          — this entity's KPI
parent.kpi.ALIAS   — parent entity's KPI
load.kpi.ALIAS     — load entity's KPI

Example — Reference a coil's efficiency in an AHU rule: 

load.kpi.COIL_EFF < 0.6

docs.title: KPI Pyramids
docs.text: The KPI Pyramid dashboard widget displays the worst-performing entities for a selected KPI across a family, making it easy to identify outliers in large portfolios with many entities.

docs.title: Enabling and Disabling
docs.text: Like rules, KPI calculations can be disabled at the family or entity level to reduce computation on inactive or excluded entities.

docs.title: Optimization Hints
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What are Optimization Hints?
docs.text:
Optimization hints are energy-efficiency recommendations linked to entity families. They highlight common improvement opportunities for a given equipment type and guide analysts toward corrective measures.

Hints are pre-defined suggestions — they do not automatically detect a condition, but rather serve as a structured checklist of known optimization strategies for each family type.

docs.title: Accessing Optimization Hints
docs.text: Optimization hints are available in Modeling → Families, on the configuration page for each family. They can also be surfaced in dashboard widgets for review by technical teams.

docs.title: Using Hints in Practice
docs.text:
Optimization hints are typically reviewed during analysis sessions. When a hint is relevant:

  1. Investigate the corresponding data points and rules in the Explorer or Query Page
  2. If an issue is confirmed, create a Task to track the corrective measure
  3. Link the task to the affected entity for traceability

docs.title: Rules
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What is a Rule?
docs.text: A rule is a formula defined on a family that evaluates to 1 (fault condition) or 0 (normal) for each entity in that family, calculated every hour. When a rule returns 1, the corresponding entity appears in the Fault Detection module.

docs.title: Writing Rules
docs.text:
Rules are written in the Query Page using the formula syntax. A rule should express the abnormal condition — when it is true, a fault is detected.

Example — Supply air temperature too high: 

tag.SA_T.avg > tag.SA_SP.avg + 3
Returns 1 when the average supply air temperature exceeds the setpoint by more than 3°C.

Example — Fan running outside occupied hours: 

tag.FAN_STATUS.avg > 0.5 AND schedule() = 0
Returns 1 when the fan is on during unoccupied hours.

docs.title: Rule Configuration
docs.text:
Each rule has:

Field Description Name Displayed in the Fault Detection module Alias Short code for formula reference Formula The fault condition expression Priority 0–5; controls color coding in fault detection (red = 0–1, orange = 2–3, yellow = 4–5) Email notification Optional; triggers an alert when a fault is detected

docs.title: Enabling and Disabling Rules
docs.text: Rules can be toggled at the family level (applies to all entities) or at the individual entity level (e.g., to suppress known false positives on a specific unit while keeping the rule active for others).

docs.title: Rule Calculation Timing
docs.text: All rules run hourly. There is no on-demand recalculation — results from the previous hour are always displayed.

docs.title: Building Configuration
docs.text: Building configuration is the first step in any Dybee implementation. Settings entered here are used throughout the platform — for energy calculations, weather data, and cost analysis.

docs.title: Required Settings
docs.text:
Navigate to Administration → Building Settings to configure:

Setting Description Building name Display name across all modules Floor area Used for energy intensity calculations (kWh/m²) Currency Applied to all cost calculations Latitude / Longitude Used to retrieve weather data from NASA

docs.title: Weather Data
docs.text:
Dybee automatically retrieves outdoor temperature and climate data from NASA using the building's coordinates. This data feeds into:

  • Baseline regression models
  • Degree-day calculations
  • The Temperature widget on dashboards

Accurate coordinates are required for weather data to function correctly.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Once the building is configured:

  1. Add users and assign roles
  2. Set up energy sources

docs.title: User Management
docs.text: Users are managed under Administration → Users. Each user is assigned a role that controls which modules they can view and edit.

docs.title: Access Levels
docs.text:
Dybee uses role-based access control. Higher-privilege roles include access to:

  • Building Administration (configuration, user management)
  • Data Acquisition (BACnet point mapping, Dybee-Link deployment)
  • Modeling (entity creation, family configuration)

Standard users have read and write access to operational modules (dashboards, explorer, tasks, reports) but cannot modify the building model or system configuration.

Tip

Assign administration roles only to the analysts or engineers responsible for the building model. Energy managers typically operate with standard user access.

docs.title: What is a Dashboard?
docs.text:
A dashboard is a configurable monitoring page composed of widgets. It provides a synthesized view of operational data, energy performance, fault status, and task progress for a building.

Each user has a personal dashboard and can access the building's default dashboard and dashboards created by teammates.

docs.title: Grid Layout
docs.text:
Dashboards use a grid-based layout:

  • 4 columns × 4 rows per page
  • Widgets are 1–4 columns wide and 1 row tall
  • Multiple pages can be added to a single dashboard for organizing different analyses

docs.title: Pages
docs.text:
A dashboard can have multiple pages. Each page is configured independently with:

Setting Description Title Label shown in the page tab Auto-load Whether the page loads automatically (disable for resource-heavy pages) Maximum entities Limits automatic loading of large families Maximum time range Prevents overly long calculation windows Family parameters Default parameter context for entity-targeted widgets Page order Controls tab ordering

docs.title: Adding Widgets
docs.text: Click the + button to add a widget to a page. Select the widget type, configure it, and position it on the grid. See Widget Reference for all available widget types.

docs.title: Dashboard Selection
docs.text:
Use the dropdown at the top of the Dashboard module to switch between:

  • Your personal dashboard
  • The building's default dashboard
  • A teammate's dashboard (read-only)

docs.title: Importing Pages from Other Users
docs.text:
Dashboard pages can be imported from teammates within the same building or from other buildings you have access to.

To import a page:

  1. Open your dashboard in edit mode
  2. Select Import Page
  3. Choose the source: same building or another building
  4. Select the page to import
  5. Review any compatibility warnings
  6. Save to complete the import

docs.title: Compatibility Warnings
docs.text:
When importing a page from a different building, Dybee checks whether the source building's modeling is compatible with the target building:

  • Missing entities or families — a widget targeting an entity that doesn't exist in the target building will be flagged
  • Missing data points — widgets referencing acquisition points that aren't available will not display data
  • Missing KPIs or rules — formula-based widgets may fail if the target model doesn't have the same KPIs defined

Warning

Always review compatibility warnings before saving an imported page. Importing without resolving incompatibilities will result in empty or broken widgets.

docs.title: Duplicating Pages
docs.text:
Any dashboard page (including imported ones) can be duplicated. Duplicates are fully editable and are independent of the source page — changes to the original do not affect the copy.

This is the recommended approach when you want to use an existing page as a starting point but need to adapt it for a specific building.

docs.title: What is a Dashboard Template?
docs.text:
A template is a dashboard page that acts as a master configuration. Child dashboards across other buildings inherit the template's layout and widgets. When the parent template is updated, all linked child pages update automatically.

This enables centralized management of standardized dashboards across large portfolios without manually updating each building.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
For templates to work correctly across buildings:

  • All buildings must follow the same modeling conventions — identical tag names, KPI aliases, and parameter names
  • For example, outdoor air temperature must be mapped to tag OA_T in every building where the template is deployed

Inconsistent modeling will cause template widgets to fail or display no data in buildings that don't match the expected structure.

docs.title: Creating a Template
docs.text:
1. Build the dashboard page on the parent building site
  2. Open Page Settings (edit mode)
  3. Enable the Template option
  4. Save

docs.title: Deploying a Template to a Child Building
docs.text:
1. Log into the child building site
  2. Open the Dashboard module
  3. Add a new page and select the template from the available list
  4. Save

Child pages are read-only — they cannot be edited directly. To customize a child page for a specific building, duplicate the page first, then modify the copy independently (it will no longer receive parent updates).

docs.title: Identifying the Parent
docs.text: To find which parent template generated a child page, open the page's edit function — it displays the parent site name and page name.

docs.title: Widget Reference
docs.text: Widgets are organized into six categories. All widgets require a title and a width (1–4 grid cells). An optional hyperlink can be added to any widget.

docs.title: General
docs.text: Widget Description Image Displays a building photo or custom image Temperature Live NASA weather data: outdoor temperature, humidity, wind speed. Requires building coordinates. Notes Free text block for instructions, context, or documentation Carousel Rotating image slideshow

docs.title: Energy
docs.text:
Energy widgets require completed invoice entry and a baseline model in Energy Monitoring → Measurement & Verification.

Widget Description Performance Tracking Actual vs. predicted consumption per period Savings Monitoring Savings in %, CO₂, and currency vs. baseline Comparative Analysis Building performance vs. benchmarks Cumulative Energy 12-month rolling consumption trend Energy Results Table Comprehensive exportable summary table

docs.title: Operation
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

Operation widgets require acquired data points and modeling (Analytics Plan).

Widget Description Graphics Module Embeds a chart created in the Visualization module Temporal Comparison Overlays data from two time periods Point Lists Latest values with min/max/avg/total statistics Explorer Links to a saved point set in the Explorer module KPI Pie Chart Proportional breakdown of KPI values across entities KPI Pyramid Worst-performing entities for a selected KPI Value Display Single point value with trend indicator Gauge Current value or 24-hour average displayed as a gauge Data Table Hourly matrix with aggregation options

docs.title: Management
docs.text: Widget Description Task Summary Count of tasks by status Task Tracking Monthly rates for task creation, acceptance, and completion Fault Detection Auto-detected faults from the last 24 hours Optimization Measures Recommendations for technical teams

docs.title: Module
docs.text: Widget Description Comparative Analysis Actual performance vs. AI-generated reference model with acceptable limits

docs.title: Other
docs.text: Widget Description Fault Detection Summary Numerical fault count organized by priority level

docs.title: Using Widgets from Other Users
docs.text: The Other Users tab in the widget picker allows you to import and reuse widgets created by teammates. This is useful for standardizing views across team members on the same building.

docs.title: Data Acquisition — Overview & Prerequisites
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

Data acquisition requires the Analytics Plan and Dybee-Link hardware.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Dybee acquires real-time operational data from building systems via the BACnet protocol. An on-site hardware gateway — the Dybee-Link mini-PC — communicates with the building's BMS and forwards data to the Dybee cloud.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before the acquisition interface becomes accessible:

  1. BGI completes baseline site configuration — the building model structure must be in place before points can be mapped to entities.
  2. Dybee-Link is installed on-site — the mini-PC must be physically installed and connected to the building network.
  3. IT coordination is complete — the Dybee-Link requires network access to the BACnet devices and outbound internet connectivity to the Dybee cloud.

docs.title: Acquisition Flow
docs.text:
BACnet Devices → Dybee-Link (on-site) → Dybee Cloud → Entity Operations

Once configured, the Dybee-Link continuously polls the selected BACnet points at defined intervals and logs historical data to the cloud.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• BACnet Connection — discover devices and objects
  • Point Configuration — select points, set intervals, and deploy

docs.title: BACnet Connection
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: Device Discovery
docs.text:
Once the Dybee-Link is online, navigate to Administration → Data Acquisition to begin the BACnet setup.

Step 1 — Discover devices: Dybee scans the BACnet network and lists all visible controllers and devices. Select the devices that contain the points you want to acquire.

Step 2 — Acquire BACnet objects: For each selected device, Dybee retrieves the list of available BACnet objects (Analog Input, Binary Input, Analog Value, etc.).

docs.title: Filtering Points
docs.text:
The full point list from a BMS can contain thousands of objects. Use the filter tools to narrow down to relevant points:

  • Filter by device
  • Filter by object type
  • Filter by name pattern

Select only the points needed for your entities. Unnecessary points increase network load and data storage without analytical value.

docs.title: BACnet Write
docs.text: Dybee supports BACnet write operations in addition to reads, allowing setpoint adjustments or command triggers directly from the platform when write access is granted by the BMS.

docs.title: Point Configuration & Deployment
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: Selecting Points
docs.text:
From the filtered BACnet object list, select the points to acquire. For each point, configure:

Setting Description Acquisition interval How frequently the point is polled (e.g., every 5 minutes) Precision Number of decimal places stored Historical logging Enable to store trend data for analysis

Choose acquisition intervals based on the point's purpose:

  • Status and setpoints — 15–30 minute intervals are typically sufficient
  • Energy and power readings — 15-minute intervals for demand analysis
  • Control sequences — 5-minute or shorter for fault detection accuracy

docs.title: Deploying to Dybee-Link
docs.text:
Once points are configured, the settings must be deployed to the on-site Dybee-Link mini-PC.

  1. Review the pending configuration changes
  2. Click Deploy
  3. The Dybee-Link downloads the new configuration and begins acquiring the selected points

Warning

Deployment pushes changes to the physical Dybee-Link device. Verify the configuration before deploying, especially acquisition intervals, as high-frequency polling of many points can increase network load.

docs.title: After Deployment
docs.text:
Acquired points become available in:

  • Explorer — for real-time and historical trend visualization
  • Entity Operations — for assignment to entities in the digital twin
  • Formulas — as {point_name} references in independent formulas

docs.title: Energy Monitoring — Overview
docs.text: Energy monitoring is available on all plans and covers the full lifecycle of energy performance tracking: from raw invoice data to savings verification.

docs.title: What You Can Do
docs.text:
• Track energy consumption across multiple sources (electricity, gas, water, etc.)
  • Build IPMVP-compliant baseline models for savings measurement
  • Calculate and report energy savings, cost savings, and GHG reductions
  • Analyze power demand peaks for tariff optimization
  • Compare buildings across a portfolio

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text: Building config → Energy sources → Invoice data → Baseline model → Savings analysis

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
• Energy Sources — set up energy types and units
  • Invoice Data — enter consumption data manually, via Excel, or automatically
  • Baseline Modeling — create IPMVP regression models
  • Savings & Cost Analysis — interpret results and cost calculation methods

docs.title: What is a Baseline Model?
docs.text:
A baseline model is a statistical regression that predicts expected energy consumption based on influence factors — variables that drive consumption independently of operational efficiency. Comparing actual consumption against the predicted baseline quantifies the impact of energy interventions.

Dybee uses IPMVP-compliant baseline modeling (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol).

docs.title: Influence Factors
docs.text:
Common factors used in baseline models:

Factor Notes Outdoor temperature Retrieved automatically from NASA using building coordinates Heating degree days (HDD) Derived from temperature data Cooling degree days (CDD) Derived from temperature data Occupancy Manual schedule or imported data

Dybee uses linear regression to fit the baseline. The model is evaluated over a reference period — typically 12–24 months of pre-intervention data.

docs.title: Creating a Baseline
docs.text:
Navigate to Energy Monitoring → Measurement & Verification:

  1. Select the energy source
  2. Define the reference period (pre-intervention date range)
  3. Select influence factors to include in the regression
  4. Review the model quality indicators (R², residuals)
  5. Save the baseline

docs.title: Interpreting Model Quality
docs.text:
A well-fitted baseline should have:

  • R² > 0.75 for weather-sensitive buildings (heating/cooling dominated)
  • Residuals distributed randomly with no strong seasonal pattern
  • No obvious outliers distorting the regression

If the model quality is low, consider adjusting the reference period or adding/removing influence factors.

docs.title: Post-Intervention Period
docs.text: Once a baseline exists, Dybee continuously compares actual consumption to the model prediction. The difference represents measured savings (or regression).

docs.title: Data Entry Methods
docs.text: Dybee supports three ways to import consumption data for each energy source.

docs.title: Manual Entry
docs.text:
Enter billing period data directly in the interface:

  • Start date
  • End date
  • Consumption value

Suitable for small datasets or one-time corrections.

docs.title: Excel Import
docs.text:
Import multiple periods at once using a spreadsheet with three columns:

Date Start | Date End | Value

Dates must follow the format expected by Dybee (verify in the import dialog). This is the most efficient method for entering historical data.

docs.title: Automatic Retrieval
docs.text: For supported utility providers, Dybee can retrieve invoice data automatically using your utility account credentials. Once configured, new billing data is imported without manual intervention.

docs.title: Data Quality
docs.text:
Baseline models and savings calculations are only as accurate as the underlying consumption data. Before creating a baseline:

  • Ensure all billing periods are entered without gaps
  • Verify that unusual values (e.g., estimated bills, corrections) are flagged or adjusted
  • Confirm that the "Include in total consumption" flag is set on all relevant sources

docs.title: Cost Calculation Methods
docs.text: Dybee offers two methods for translating energy savings into cost savings. Choose the method that matches how your client's tariff structure works.

docs.title: Facturation (Invoice) Method
docs.text:
• Reference cost = the actual invoice amount for the period
  • Predicted cost = average unit cost × model-predicted consumption

Use this method when the primary goal is to verify savings against actual billed amounts and the tariff structure is relatively stable.

docs.title: Unit Cost Method
docs.text:
• Both reference and predicted costs use a fixed unit price
  • Recommended when analyzing both consumption and power demand separately

Tip

Use the Unit Cost Method when your client pays demand charges. It allows you to separate consumption savings from peak demand savings.

docs.title: Power Peak Analysis
docs.text:
Dybee analyzes power demand at 15-minute intervals to identify peak demand events. This supports:

  • Identifying spikes that trigger demand surcharges
  • Quantifying the cost impact of demand reduction measures
  • Monitoring progress after peak shaving interventions

Power peak analysis is available under Energy Monitoring → Peak Demand.

docs.title: Portfolio Comparison
docs.text:
The Portfolio module shows a comparative view across all buildings you have access to. Metrics available for comparison:

  • Energy intensity (kWh/m²)
  • Savings percentage vs. baseline
  • CO₂ emissions
  • Energy Star ratings (where applicable)

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text: Energy monitoring results integrate directly into the dashboard widgets (Performance Tracking, Savings Monitoring, Cumulative Energy) and can be included in formal reports. See Widget Reference for energy widget details.

docs.title: What is an Energy Source?
docs.text: An energy source represents a utility meter or energy type for a building — electricity, natural gas, district heating, water, etc. Each source collects consumption data and feeds into the building's energy totals.

docs.title: Creating an Energy Source
docs.text:
Navigate to Energy Monitoring → Sources and create an entry for each meter or utility type:

Field Description Name Label for the source (e.g., "Main Electricity Meter") Energy type Electricity, gas, thermal, water, etc. Unit Native billing unit (kWh, m³, GJ, etc.) Include in total consumption Must be enabled for the source to count toward building totals

Warning

The Include in total consumption checkbox must be enabled. Sources without it checked are excluded from all consumption totals and dashboard widgets.

docs.title: Multiple Sources
docs.text: A building can have multiple sources of the same energy type (e.g., two electricity meters). Each is tracked independently and combined in the total when both are included.

docs.title: Units and Conversions
docs.text:
Dybee automatically converts all sources to a common set of display units for reporting:

  • Native units (as entered)
  • kWh equivalent
  • GJ
  • CO₂ (kg)
  • Cost (in the building's configured currency)

docs.title: Fault Detection
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

Fault detection requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Fault Detection module surfaces active rule violations across all modeled entities. Rules run hourly — when a rule returns 1 for an entity, that fault appears here until it is resolved or acknowledged.

Navigate to Analysis → Fault Detection.

docs.title: Filtering Faults
docs.text:
Use the filter bar to narrow the fault list:

Filter Options Entity scope Specific component, full family, or all entities Status Handled / unhandled Text search Filter by entity name or rule name

docs.title: Priority Color Coding
docs.text:
Color Priority Level Red 0–1 (critical) Orange 2–3 (high) Yellow 4–5 (medium) Green Addressed / resolved

Priority is set when the rule is defined. See Rules for configuration details.

docs.title: Actions per Fault
docs.text:
For each detected fault, you can:

Action Description View rule details Inspect the formula and its recent evaluation history Create a task Open a corrective commissioning task linked to this fault and entity Open Explorer Jump to a point-level trend view for the affected entity Open Query Page Run ad-hoc formula analysis on the entity's data Toggle email alerts Enable or disable email notification for this rule on this entity

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
The recommended fault investigation flow:

  1. Filter to unhandled, high-priority faults
  2. Review the rule details to understand the condition
  3. Open the Explorer to examine the relevant data points
  4. If the fault is confirmed: create a corrective task
  5. If the fault is a false positive: disable the rule for that entity or adjust the rule threshold

docs.title: Formulas & Calculations — Overview
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

Formulas and calculations require the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What are Formulas Used For?
docs.text:
Formulas in Dybee are used to:

  • Calculate virtual points from raw data (e.g., derived efficiency metrics)
  • Define rules for automated fault detection
  • Define KPIs for performance tracking
  • Perform ad-hoc analysis in the Query Page

docs.title: Formula Editor
docs.text: The primary environment for writing and testing formulas is the Query Page, accessible from the Analysis module. It allows you to write a formula, select a time range, and validate the result before saving it as a rule or KPI.

docs.title: Formula Syntax
docs.text:
Formulas follow a syntax similar to Excel but leverage the building model hierarchy. Two types exist:

  • Independent formulas — reference raw BACnet points directly
  • Targeted formulas — navigate the entity model using relationships

See Formula Types for details, and the Functions Reference for the complete list of available operators and functions.

docs.title: Functions Reference
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: Arithmetic Operators
docs.text: Operator Description + - * / Basic arithmetic ^ Exponentiation

docs.title: Comparison & Logic
docs.text: Operator Description < > <= >= = <> Comparison operators AND OR Boolean logic

docs.title: Mathematical Functions
docs.text: Function Description sqrt(x) Square root log(x) Base-10 logarithm ln(x) Natural logarithm sin(x) cos(x) tan(x) Trigonometric functions asin(x) acos(x) atan(x) Inverse trigonometric functions

docs.title: Aggregation Functions
docs.text: Function Description avg(a, b, ...) Average of multiple values max(a, b, ...) Maximum of multiple values min(a, b, ...) Minimum of multiple values

docs.title: Conditional
docs.text:
Function Description if(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false) Conditional expression

Example: 

if(tag.SA_T.avg > 25, 1, 0)

docs.title: Scheduling
docs.text: Function Description schedule() Returns 1 during occupied hours, 0 otherwise, based on the building's schedule configuration

docs.title: Counting
docs.text: Function Description count(condition, ...) Counts arguments that meet the condition nb(a, b, ...) Counts non-null values

docs.title: Time Shift
docs.text:
Function Description shift(value, n) Returns the value of value at t ± n hours

Example: shift(tag.SA_T.avg, -1) returns last hour's supply air temperature.

docs.title: Interpolation
docs.text: Function Description curve(x, x1, y1, x2, y2, ...) Interpolates y for a given x along a piecewise linear curve

docs.title: Selection
docs.text: Function Description first(a, b, ...) Returns the first non-null value from the list

docs.title: Alias X
docs.text: Alias X is a special mechanism for writing rules that work across equipment families with different topologies. Instead of hardcoding a specific hierarchy path, you assign Alias X (load.X or source.X) to different entity types depending on the family. This makes the rule generalize cleanly across buildings where the same equipment may be connected differently.

docs.title: Model Hierarchy Navigation
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Targeted formulas can navigate the energy flow relationships in the building model. This allows a single rule or KPI to reference connected entities without hardcoding specific names.

docs.title: Navigation Functions
docs.text: Function Description parent The equipment entity that contains this component child A component entity contained within this equipment source The entity supplying energy to this entity load The entity receiving energy from this entity firstLoad The first entity in the downstream (load) hierarchy firstSource The first entity in the upstream (source) hierarchy anyLoad All entities in the full downstream hierarchy; returns their average anySource All entities in the full upstream hierarchy; returns their average anyLoadSib Sibling entities sharing the same load connection anySourceSib Sibling entities sharing the same source connection

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Reference a parent entity's tag: 

parent.tag.OA_T.avg

Reference a load entity's KPI: 

load.kpi.EFFICIENCY

Check if any downstream entity has a fault: 

anyLoad.kpi.FAULT_RULE > 0

docs.title: Standard Tag Aliases
docs.text:
These tag names are used by convention in the Factory Library and BGI-supplied families:

Alias Description OA_T Outdoor air temperature SA_T Supply air temperature RA_T Return air temperature AHU Air handling unit reference HC Heating coil T Generic temperature sensor

Following these conventions ensures that imported Factory Library rules and templates work without modification.

docs.title: Formula Types
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: Independent Formulas
docs.text:
Independent formulas reference raw BACnet acquisition points directly, without using the model hierarchy. Point names are wrapped in curly braces.

Syntax: {point_name}

Example: 

({Supply_Air_Temp} - {Return_Air_Temp}) * {Air_Flow_Rate} * 1.2

Use independent formulas when:

  • You need a simple calculation from raw points
  • The formula does not need to generalize across multiple entities
  • You are working outside the context of a family rule or KPI

docs.title: Targeted Formulas
docs.text:
Targeted formulas reference entities in the building model and navigate their relationships. Entity names are wrapped in square brackets.

Syntax: [EntityName].tag.TAGNAME or [EntityName].kpi.ALIAS

Example: 

[AHU-01].tag.SA_T - [AHU-01].tag.OA_T

Use targeted formulas in rules and KPIs, where the formula must work generically across all entities in a family. In that context, you use hierarchy functions instead of a specific entity name — the formula is evaluated in the context of each entity.

Example rule (applies to every AHU in the family): 

tag.SA_T > tag.OA_T + 5

docs.title: Hourly Aggregation
docs.text:
Raw data points are acquired at sub-hourly intervals. When used in rules and KPIs (which calculate hourly), append an aggregation suffix:

Suffix Description .avg Hourly average .max Hourly maximum .min Hourly minimum .tot Hourly total (sum)

Example: tag.SA_T.avg — average supply air temperature for the hour.

docs.title: What is Dybee?
docs.text: Dybee is a cloud-based platform for continuous building commissioning (CxC). It helps teams maintain optimal building performance over time by combining energy monitoring, system modeling, real-time data acquisition, and automated fault detection.

docs.title: Implementation Methodology
docs.text:
Dybee is structured around four implementation stages. How far you progress depends on your subscription plan.

Stage Description Plan 1 Building configuration and user management All plans 2 Energy monitoring — baseline modeling and intervention tracking All plans 3 Digital twin modeling, Dybee-Link installation, and data acquisition Analytics Plan 4 Fault detection, optimization analysis, and task management Analytics Plan

docs.title: Core Principle: Continuous Commissioning
docs.text: Unlike a one-time optimization, Dybee is designed for ongoing monitoring. The platform supports iterative analysis cycles — detect a drift, create a corrective task, track resolution, repeat. This methodology is referred to throughout the documentation as CxC (continuous commissioning).

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text: Understanding these terms is essential before working with Dybee's modeling and analysis features.

docs.title: Structural Framework
docs.text: Dybee organizes building systems in a hierarchy. At the top is the building, which contains families, which group entities.

docs.title: Entity
docs.text:
An entity represents a single building system component — an air handling unit, a pump, a coil, a sensor, etc. Entities are the core unit of the Dybee model. Each entity has:

  • Associated data points (operational readings from BACnet)
  • Tags and parameters for cross-entity standardization
  • Linked rules, KPIs, tasks, notes, and files

docs.title: Family
docs.text:
A family is a group of similar entities. Families define the rules, KPIs, and templates that apply to all their member entities. There are four family types:

Type Description Building Top-level grouping representing the facility Equipment Major systems (e.g., AHU, chiller, boiler) Component Sub-systems within equipment (e.g., heating coil, fan) Instrument Individual sensors and measurement devices

docs.title: Energy Flow
docs.text: An energy flow is a directional relationship between entities, indicating which entity supplies energy to which. Flows are used in formula hierarchy navigation (source, load) and in energy modeling.

docs.title: Rule
docs.text: A rule is a logical formula that evaluates to 0 (normal) or 1 (fault). Rules run hourly against all entities in their family and trigger alerts in the Fault Detection module when the result is 1.

docs.title: KPI
docs.text: A KPI (Key Performance Indicator) is a quantifiable metric calculated per entity. KPIs can reference rules, data points, and other KPIs, and are reusable in formulas via [Entity].kpi.ALIAS.

docs.title: Digital Twin
docs.text: The digital twin is the complete model of a building's electromechanical systems — its entities, their relationships, their data points, and their behavioral rules. Stages 3 and 4 of the implementation methodology build and operate the digital twin.

docs.title: Baseline Model
docs.text: A statistical regression model that predicts expected energy consumption based on influence factors (outdoor temperature, degree days, occupancy). Used to calculate savings by comparing actual vs. predicted consumption.

docs.title: IPMVP
docs.text: The International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol. Dybee uses IPMVP-compliant baseline modeling for energy savings measurement and verification.

docs.title: Interface Layout
docs.text:
The Dybee interface has two main navigation areas:

  • Left sidebar — main module navigation
  • Top-right menu — user account, building selection, settings

docs.title: Main Modules
docs.text: Module Description Portfolio Multi-building overview and comparison Dashboard Configurable widget-based monitoring pages Energy Monitoring Invoice management, baseline modeling, savings tracking Explorer Real-time and historical data browsing Visualization Chart creation and management Reports Automated reporting Analysis Fault detection, rules, KPIs, and query tool Tasks Optimization measure tracking Modeling Entity management — Entities, Families, Comments, Logbook Administration Building config, users, data acquisition

docs.title: Access Levels
docs.text: Module visibility and edit permissions are controlled by user roles. Some modules (Modeling, Administration, Data Acquisition) are restricted to users with elevated permissions. See Users & Permissions.

docs.title: Subscription Plans
docs.text: Dybee is available in two plans. Features in this documentation are marked where a specific plan is required.

docs.title: Energy Plan
docs.text:
Covers stages 1 and 2 of the implementation methodology.

Included features:

  • Building configuration and user management
  • Energy source setup and invoice management
  • Baseline modeling and savings calculation (IPMVP)
  • Portfolio-level building comparison
  • Dashboards, widgets, and reports
  • Project documentation (logbook, comments, tasks)

docs.title: Analytics Plan
docs.text:
Covers all four stages. Includes everything in the Energy Plan, plus:

  • BMS integration via Dybee-Link (BACnet)
  • Real-time data acquisition and point mapping
  • Digital twin modeling (entities, families, energy flows)
  • Automated rules and fault detection
  • KPIs and formula calculations
  • Optimization hints

Analytics Plan

Features that require the Analytics Plan are marked with this notice throughout the documentation.

docs.title: Digital Twin Overview
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

Building modeling requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What is the Digital Twin?
docs.text:
The digital twin is a structured software representation of a building's electromechanical systems. In Dybee, it consists of:

  • Entities — individual systems and components (AHUs, pumps, coils, sensors)
  • Families — groups of similar entities that share rules, KPIs, and templates
  • Energy flows — directional relationships between entities indicating energy transfer

The digital twin is the foundation for all analytical features: real-time data visualization, fault detection, KPI calculation, and formula-based analysis.

docs.title: Modeling Workflow
docs.text:
Build the model in this order:

  1. Define families — import from the Factory Library or create from scratch; configure tags, parameters, and templates
  2. Create entities — instantiate individual systems as members of their respective families
  3. Associate data points — link BACnet acquisition points to entity operations
  4. Map energy flows — establish source/load relationships between entities
  5. Enable rules and KPIs — activate fault detection and performance monitoring

See the following pages for details on each step:

  • Families & Templates
  • Entities
  • Energy Flows

docs.title: Energy Flows
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What is an Energy Flow?
docs.text:
An energy flow is a directional relationship between two entities indicating that one supplies energy to the other. Flows are used to:

  • Represent the physical topology of a building's systems
  • Enable hierarchy navigation in formulas (source, load, parent, child)
  • Support energy flow analysis in dashboards and reports

docs.title: Source and Load
docs.text:
Each flow has a direction:

  • The source entity supplies energy (e.g., a boiler supplying hot water)
  • The load entity receives energy (e.g., a heating coil receiving hot water from the boiler)

In formulas, you navigate these relationships using source and load functions. See Model Hierarchy Navigation for the full syntax.

docs.title: Configuring Flows
docs.text:
Energy flows are defined in Modeling → Entities by editing an entity's relationships. Select the source or load entity from the available entity list within the building model.

Tip

Accurate energy flow mapping is important for formulas that traverse the model hierarchy. Incomplete flows may cause formula errors or unexpected results in rules and KPIs.

docs.title: Entities
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What is an Entity?
docs.text: An entity is a single instance of a building system — one specific AHU, one pump, one temperature sensor. Entities are members of a family and inherit its rules, KPIs, and template structure.

docs.title: Creating Entities
docs.text:
Navigate to Modeling → Entities and select the target family. For each entity:

  1. Enter a name and description
  2. Assign it to the correct family (Equipment, Component, or Instrument)
  3. Associate operational data points acquired via BACnet
  4. Assign tags to map points to their family-defined roles
  5. Set parameter values specific to this instance (setpoints, thresholds)

docs.title: Associating Data Points
docs.text:
Each entity has an Operations section listing all data points assigned to it. Points are sourced from the BACnet acquisition layer (see Data Acquisition).

When assigning a point, select the appropriate tag to tell Dybee what that point represents in the context of the family's rules and KPIs.

docs.title: Entity-Level Documentation
docs.text:
Each entity has dedicated spaces for:

  • Notes — free-text observations and modeling decisions
  • Tags — metadata for filtering and analysis
  • Linked tasks — commissioning measures associated with this entity
  • Comments — typed annotations (bookmark, defect, calibration, etc.)
  • Files — technical documents, plans, and datasheets

docs.title: Replicating Entities
docs.text: When multiple sections of a building contain identical equipment, entities can be replicated to avoid repetitive configuration. Review tag assignments and parameter values after replication to ensure correctness for each instance.

docs.title: Families & Templates
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

This feature requires the Analytics Plan.

docs.title: What is a Family?
docs.text:
A family is a group of entities of the same type (e.g., all air handling units in a building). Families define the rules, KPIs, tags, parameters, and templates that apply uniformly to every entity in the group.

Families are designed to manage datasets with thousands of data points across many entities without requiring per-entity configuration.

docs.title: Family Types
docs.text: Type Role in the model Building Top-level container representing the facility Equipment Major mechanical systems (AHU, chiller, boiler, etc.) Component Sub-systems within equipment (heating coil, fan, damper) Instrument Sensors and measurement points

docs.title: Configuration Page
docs.text:
Families are created and managed from Modeling → Families. The configuration page is the central hub for:

  • Creating and editing families
  • Managing tags and parameters
  • Defining templates (Gabarits)
  • Writing rules and KPIs
  • Enabling optimization hints

docs.title: Tags and Parameters
docs.text:
Tags are metadata labels assigned to data acquisition points within an entity. They standardize point naming across all entities in a family, enabling rules and KPIs to reference OA_T (outdoor air temperature) rather than a device-specific point name.

Parameters are configurable values specific to each entity instance — setpoints, thresholds, design values — that rules and KPIs can reference.

docs.title: Templates (Gabarits)
docs.text:
A template is a pre-defined entity configuration for a specific equipment type. It specifies which component and instrument families the equipment should contain, which tags to expect, and how the entity should be structured.

Templates accelerate model creation when many entities share the same structure (e.g., 20 similar AHUs across a portfolio).

docs.title: Factory Library
docs.text:
The Factory Library is a collection of pre-built family configurations provided by BGI. Importing from the Factory Library gives you a ready-made set of tags, parameters, templates, rules, and KPIs for common equipment types.

To import a family:

  1. Navigate to Modeling → Families → Import
  2. Select the source: Factory Library or another project
  3. Review and confirm the import

Tip

Start with Factory Library imports where possible. Customizing an existing family is faster than building one from scratch.

docs.title: Calculation Frequency
docs.text: Rules and KPIs defined on a family calculate hourly for every entity in that family. Calculations can be disabled at the family level or for individual entities.

docs.title: Project Documentation — Overview
docs.text: Dybee includes five built-in tools for documenting project knowledge, modeling decisions, and team communications. Good documentation is essential for long-term continuous commissioning — it preserves institutional knowledge when team members change and enables multi-stakeholder coordination.

docs.title: Documentation Tools
docs.text: Tool Purpose Logbook Flexible knowledge base for modeling choices, standards, and control laws Comments & Annotations Typed annotations linked to specific entities Entity Notes & Files Per-entity free text, metadata, and file attachments Implementation Guide Macro-level progress tracker for the digital twin implementation Tasks Commissioning measure tracking (see Task Workflow)

docs.title: Entity-Level Documentation
docs.text:
Every entity in the model has its own documentation space:

  • Notes — free text for observations, calibration history, and modeling notes
  • Tags — metadata for filtering and searching across entities
  • Files — technical plans, datasheets, maintenance records, photos

Files stored at the entity level are centralized and accessible to all team members with building access.

docs.title: What are Comments?
docs.text:
Comments are typed annotations linked to specific entities in the building model. They are accessible from Modeling → Comments and from individual entity pages.

Unlike the logbook (which is building-wide), comments are attached to a specific entity and appear in its context.

docs.title: Comment Types
docs.text: Type Use Case Bookmark Mark an entity or moment of interest for follow-up Complaint Record an occupant or operational complaint linked to a system Defect Document a known equipment defect or damage Calibration Record a sensor calibration event with date and value Information General note — observations, context, reference data

docs.title: Collaboration
docs.text:
Comments are visible to and editable by all team members with building access. They appear in the entity's comment feed in chronological order.

Use comments for time-stamped, entity-specific observations — things that happened at a specific moment in the context of a specific system. Use the Logbook for broader, structural documentation.

docs.title: What is the Implementation Guide?
docs.text:
The Implementation Guide is a macro-level progress tracker for the digital twin implementation. It provides a shared view of how far along the building model is across each implementation phase, and is accessible to all stakeholders involved in the project.

Navigate to Modeling → Implementation Guide.

docs.title: What it Tracks
docs.text:
The guide tracks completion status for each phase of the implementation methodology:

Phase Description Building configuration Basic setup completed Energy monitoring Sources, invoices, baseline model Modeling Entities, families, tags, and templates Data acquisition BACnet connection, point mapping, deployment Analysis Rules, KPIs, fault detection activated

docs.title: Cooperative Space
docs.text:
The Implementation Guide includes a shared collaborative space where team members can leave notes, attach files, and communicate about implementation progress. This is distinct from the logbook — it is focused on project management rather than building knowledge.

File attachments per phase allow you to store reference documents, sign-off records, and deliverable outputs alongside the progress tracker.

docs.title: Use in Multi-Stakeholder Projects
docs.text: For projects involving multiple teams (building owner, energy manager, BMS contractor, BGI analyst), the Implementation Guide provides a single point of truth for project status that all parties can access without requiring access to the technical modeling tools.

docs.title: What is the Logbook?
docs.text: The logbook (Journal de Bord) is a structured knowledge base for the building project. It is accessible from Modeling → Logbook and is intended for information that outlives any individual task or session.

docs.title: What to Use It For
docs.text:
• Modeling decisions and their rationale (why a family was structured a certain way)
  • Control law documentation (setpoint schedules, control sequences)
  • Site-specific standards and naming conventions
  • Commissioning history and major interventions
  • Notes from site visits or BMS vendor meetings

docs.title: Format
docs.text:
The logbook supports structured text entries with headings, lists, tables, and inline images. Entries are timestamped and attributed to their author.

Tip

The logbook is the right place to document anything that a new team member would need to understand the building model and analysis methodology — context that wouldn't be obvious from the model itself.

docs.title: What is Continuous Commissioning?
docs.text:
Continuous commissioning (CxC) is the ongoing process of monitoring building performance, detecting degradation, implementing corrective measures, and verifying results — repeated over the building's lifespan.

In Dybee, CxC is supported by the combination of:

  • Fault Detection — automated identification of deviations
  • Tasks — structured tracking of corrective measures from proposal to completion
  • Project Documentation — institutional memory across team changes

docs.title: The CxC Cycle
docs.text:
Monitor → Detect → Analyze → Propose → Implement → Verify → Monitor
  1. Rules detect a fault condition in the building model
  2. The analyst investigates via the Explorer and Query Page
  3. A task is created describing the problem and proposed measure
  4. The task is assigned and executed by the technical team
  5. Post-implementation data is reviewed to verify the correction
  6. The cycle repeats

docs.title: Task Module
docs.text:
The Task module is the operational hub for tracking all commissioning measures. It is available on all plans and supports multi-stakeholder communication, document attachment, and financial impact tracking.

See Task Workflow for full details.

docs.title: Creating a Task
docs.text:
Tasks can be created from:

  • Fault Detection — via the "Create task" action on a fault card (pre-links the task to the entity and rule)
  • Tasks module — manually from the task list
  • Entity pages — directly from the entity's linked tasks section

Each task has four configuration sections:

docs.title: 1. Global Definition
docs.text: Field Description Task type Category of the measure (e.g., repair, adjustment, investigation) Start / due date Timeline for the measure Estimated impacts Expected improvements: energy savings, comfort, equipment wear, GHG reduction

docs.title: 2. Problem & Actions
docs.text:
• Description of the detected problem
  • Proposed corrective actions
  • Embedded Explorer graphics (attach trend screenshots directly)
  • File attachments

docs.title: 3. Investment & Impacts
docs.text:
• Estimated implementation cost
  • Projected energy and cost savings
  • Used for prioritization and ROI tracking across the portfolio

docs.title: 4. Documentation
docs.text:
• Historical notes and updates
  • Multi-stakeholder communication thread
  • Audit trail of actions taken

docs.title: Status Workflow
docs.text:
Pending Approval → Unassigned → Assigned → In Progress → Completed → Archived

Tasks can also be set to Refused or Deleted if they are not actionable.

docs.title: Task Views
docs.text:
The task list can be viewed in three modes:

View Shows Priority / Status Default list organized by urgency and state Impacts Sorted by estimated energy or comfort impact Financial Gains Sorted by projected cost savings

These views help prioritize which tasks to pursue when resources are limited.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Charts module allows you to create persistent, named visualizations from any combination of data points and formulas. Charts are collaborative — all users with access to the building can view and use them.

Charts can be embedded in dashboards and reports, and are accessible from the Visualization tab, the Analysis module, and directly from entity pages.

docs.title: Creating a Chart
docs.text:
Navigate to Visualization → Charts → New:

  1. Enter a title and optional description
  2. Select a chart type
  3. Add data series — select points from the Explorer or write a formula
  4. Configure filters (time range, conditional filters)
  5. Set Y-axis labels, min/max values, and multi-axis options if needed

docs.title: Chart Types
docs.text: Type Best For Line Continuous trends over time Column Periodic totals (hourly, daily) Area Volume or cumulative data Bar Horizontal comparison Scatter Plot Correlation between two variables Stepped On/off or state changes Column Stacked Multi-source contribution to a total XY Scatter Plot Regression analysis (linear, polynomial) Occurrence Frequency distribution / histogram Heatmap Hourly data across days (ideal for occupancy and usage patterns) Calendar Daily values shown across a full year Pie Chart Proportional breakdown

docs.title: Regression Analysis
docs.text: The XY Scatter Plot type supports linear and polynomial regression. Add a regression line to quantify the relationship between two variables — useful for validating baseline assumptions or analyzing equipment efficiency curves.

docs.title: Dynamic Date Ranges
docs.text: Charts support both fixed date ranges and dynamic ranges such as "last 7 days" or "last 24 hours". Dynamic ranges keep the chart current without reconfiguration, making them suitable for dashboard embedding.

docs.title: Filters
docs.text: Apply temporal filters (time of day, day of week) or conditional filters (show only data when a condition is true) to isolate specific operating modes for comparison.

docs.title: Explorer & Point Sets
docs.text:
Analytics Plan

The Explorer requires acquired data points, which are available on the Analytics Plan. The mini-explorer is available on all plans for energy data.

docs.title: What is the Explorer?
docs.text: The Explorer is Dybee's primary interface for browsing, visualizing, and analyzing time-series data. It supports real-time and historical views of any acquired BACnet point or calculated formula.

docs.title: Access Points
docs.text:
The Explorer is accessible from multiple locations:

  • Main navigation — Visualization → Explorer
  • Fault Detection module — directly from a fault card to investigate the relevant points
  • Dashboard widgets — the Explorer widget links to a pre-configured view

docs.title: Mini-Explorer
docs.text: The mini-explorer is a lightweight popup for quick inspection of a single point. It shows recent trend data without requiring a full Explorer session. Useful for rapid verification during fault investigation.

docs.title: Point Sets
docs.text:
A point set is a saved collection of points that you regularly analyze together (e.g., all temperature sensors on AHU-01). Point sets allow you to open a multi-point trend view in one click.

To create a point set:

  1. Open the Explorer
  2. Select the points you want to include
  3. Save the selection as a named point set

Point sets are personal by default but can be shared across users on the same building.

docs.title: Binary Points
docs.text: Binary (on/off) points are displayed as a status timeline rather than a numeric trend. This makes it easy to visualize equipment run times, valve states, and alarm conditions alongside analog data.

docs.title: Filters
docs.text: The Explorer supports conditional filtering to show only the data matching a specific condition — useful for isolating fault conditions or comparing behavior during occupied vs. unoccupied hours.

docs.title: Bienvenue sur Dybee
docs.text: Ceci est la documentation française de Dybee.
